first_paragraph: [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") is administratively divided into four [provinces](/wiki/Province "Province"), one [federal territory](/wiki/Federal_territory "Federal territory"), and two [disputed territories](/wiki/Territorial_dispute "Territorial dispute"): the provinces of [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_Pakistan "Punjab, Pakistan"), [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh"), [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa"), and [Balochistan](/wiki/Balochistan,_Pakistan "Balochistan, Pakistan"); the [Islamabad Capital Territory](/wiki/Islamabad_Capital_Territory "Islamabad Capital Territory"); and the administrative territories[[Note 1]](#cite_note-1) of [Azad Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Azad_Kashmir "Azad Kashmir") and [Gilgit–Baltistan](/wiki/Gilgit-Baltistan "Gilgit-Baltistan").[[Note 2]](#cite_note-5)[[4]](#cite_note-6)[[5]](#cite_note-7) As part of the [Kashmir conflict](/wiki/Kashmir_conflict "Kashmir conflict") with neighbouring [India](/wiki/India "India"), Pakistan has also claimed sovereignty over the Indian-controlled territories of [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory) "Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)") and [Ladakh](/wiki/Ladakh "Ladakh") since the [First Kashmir War](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_War_of_1947%E2%80%931948 "Indo-Pakistani War of 1947–1948") of 1947–1948. It also has a territorial dispute with India over [Junagadh](/wiki/Junagadh_State "Junagadh State"),[[6]](#cite_note-8)[[7]](#cite_note-9)[[8]](#cite_note-10)[[9]](#cite_note-11) but has never exercised administrative authority over either regions. All of Pakistan's provinces and territories are subdivided into [divisions](/wiki/Divisions_of_Pakistan "Divisions of Pakistan"), which are further subdivided into [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Pakistan "Districts of Pakistan"), and then [tehsils](/wiki/List_of_tehsils_in_Pakistan "List of tehsils in Pakistan"), which are again further subdivided into [union councils](/wiki/Union_council_(Pakistan) "Union council (Pakist

[Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") is administratively divided into four [provinces](/wiki/Province "Province"), one [federal territory](/wiki/Federal_territory "Federal territory"), and two [disputed territories](/wiki/Territorial_dispute "Territorial dispute"): the provinces of [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_Pakistan "Punjab, Pakistan"), [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh"), [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa"), and [Balochistan](/wiki/Balochistan,_Pakistan "Balochistan, Pakistan"); the [Islamabad Capital Territory](/wiki/Islamabad_Capital_Territory "Islamabad Capital Territory"); and the administrative territories[[Note 1]](#cite_note-1) of [Azad Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Azad_Kashmir "Azad Kashmir") and [Gilgit–Baltistan](/wiki/Gilgit-Baltistan "Gilgit-Baltistan").[[Note 2]](#cite_note-5)[[4]](#cite_note-6)[[5]](#cite_note-7) As part of the [Kashmir conflict](/wiki/Kashmir_conflict "Kashmir conflict") with neighbouring [India](/wiki/India "India"), Pakistan has also claimed sovereignty over the Indian-controlled territories of [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory) "Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)") and [Ladakh](/wiki/Ladakh "Ladakh") since the [First Kashmir War](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_War_of_1947%E2%80%931948 "Indo-Pakistani War of 1947–1948") of 1947–1948. It also has a territorial dispute with India over [Junagadh](/wiki/Junagadh_State "Junagadh State"),[[6]](#cite_note-8)[[7]](#cite_note-9)[[8]](#cite_note-10)[[9]](#cite_note-11) but has never exercised administrative authority over either regions. All of Pakistan's provinces and territories are subdivided into [divisions](/wiki/Divisions_of_Pakistan "Divisions of Pakistan"), which are further subdivided into [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Pakistan "Districts of Pakistan"), and then [tehsils](/wiki/List_of_tehsils_in_Pakistan "List of tehsils in Pakistan"), which are again further subdivided into [union councils](/wiki/Union_council_(Pakistan) "Union council (Pakistan)").[[10]](#cite_note-12)

[West Pakistan](/wiki/West_Pakistan "West Pakistan") (pale yellow) as it was at the time of independence, with the independent princely states of 1947 in purple

[Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") inherited the territory comprising its current provinces from [India](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") following the [Partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") on [14 August 1947](/wiki/Independence_Day_(Pakistan) "Independence Day (Pakistan)"). Two days after independence, the [Muslim](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims")-majority [Murshidabad district](/wiki/Murshidabad_district "Murshidabad district") in [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal") was moved out of the [Dominion of Pakistan](/wiki/Dominion_of_Pakistan "Dominion of Pakistan") and put within the [Dominion of India](/wiki/Dominion_of_India "Dominion of India") due to a boundary adjustment by the [Radcliffe Commission](/wiki/Radcliffe_Line "Radcliffe Line") which was aimed at keeping the [Hooghly River](/wiki/Hooghly_River "Hooghly River") entirely within India.[[11]](#cite_note-Murshidabad_Govt_Website-13)[[12]](#cite_note-14) At its inception, Pakistan consisted of two wings, which were separated from each other by around 1,600 kilometres (990 mi) of Indian territory. The western wing consisted of a merger of the [North-West Frontier Province](/wiki/North-West_Frontier_Province "North-West Frontier Province"), [West Punjab](/wiki/West_Punjab "West Punjab"), [Sind Province](/wiki/Sind_Province_(1936%E2%80%9355) "Sind Province (1936–55)"), and [Baluchistan CCP](/wiki/Baluchistan_(Chief_Commissioner%27s_Province) "Baluchistan (Chief Commissioner's Province)"). The eastern wing consisted of [East Bengal](/wiki/East_Bengal "East Bengal"). What later became the [Princely states of Pakistan](/wiki/Princely_states_of_Pakistan "Princely states of Pakistan") chose at first to remain independent.

In 1948, [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi") was separated from Sind Province to form the [Federal Capital Territory](/wiki/Federal_Capital_Territory_(Pakistan) "Federal Capital Territory (Pakistan)"). In 1950, the North-West Frontier Province absorbed the princely states of [Amb](/wiki/Amb_(princely_state) "Amb (princely state)") and [Phulra](/wiki/Phulra "Phulra") while West Punjab (designated 'West' to distinguish it from India's [Punjab in the east](/wiki/East_Punjab "East Punjab")) was renamed to simply [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_Pakistan "Punjab, Pakistan"). In 1952, the four princely states in the southwest formed the [Baluchistan States Union](/wiki/Baluchistan_States_Union "Baluchistan States Union").

In 1955, the *[One Unit](/wiki/One_Unit "One Unit")* policy was launched by then-[Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Pakistan "Prime Minister of Pakistan") [Muhammad Ali Bogra](/wiki/Mohammad_Ali_Bogra "Mohammad Ali Bogra"), whereby all the provinces and princely states of the western wing were merged to form the provincial wing of [West Pakistan](/wiki/West_Pakistan "West Pakistan"), with [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore") serving as its provincial capital. Simultaneously, East Bengal was redesignated as [East Pakistan](/wiki/East_Pakistan "East Pakistan"), with [Dacca](/wiki/Dhaka "Dhaka") serving as its provincial capital. The One Unit policy aimed to reduce expenditure and to eliminate provincial prejudices, but the [military coup of 1958](/wiki/1958_Pakistani_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1958 Pakistani coup d'état") brought difficulties when the [first military President](/wiki/Military_coups_in_Pakistan "Military coups in Pakistan"), [Ayub Khan](/wiki/Ayub_Khan_(general) "Ayub Khan (general)"), abolished the office of the [Chief Minister of West Pakistan in favour of Governor rule](/wiki/West_Pakistan#Governor_and_chief_minister "West Pakistan").

On 7 September 1958, after four years of negotiations (including six months of intense negotiations), Pakistan purchased the [enclave](/wiki/Enclave_and_exclave "Enclave and exclave") of [Gwadar](/wiki/Gwadar "Gwadar") from [Oman](/wiki/Muscat_and_Oman "Muscat and Oman") for [₨.](/wiki/Pakistani_rupee "Pakistani rupee")5.5 billion ([US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")3 million; approximately $22,410,311.42 in 2017).[[13]](#cite_note-15) Gwadar formally became a part of Pakistan on 8 December 1958, ending 174 years of Omani rule. In 1960, the federal capital was moved from Karachi to [Rawalpindi](/wiki/Rawalpindi "Rawalpindi") and in 1961, the Federal Capital Territory was also merged into West Pakistan. In 1966, the capital was again moved to the newly constructed city of [Islamabad](/wiki/Islamabad "Islamabad"). In 1962, Dacca was made the legislative capital of the country due to East Pakistan's high population.[[14]](#cite_note-16) Following the [1963 Sino–Pakistan Agreement](/wiki/Sino-Pakistan_Agreement "Sino-Pakistan Agreement"), a part of the [Gilgit Agency](/wiki/Gilgit_Agency "Gilgit Agency") (controlled by Pakistan since the [First Kashmir War](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_War_of_1947%E2%80%931948 "Indo-Pakistani War of 1947–1948")) was formally relinquished by Pakistan to the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/China "China") (the [Trans-Karakoram Tract/Shaksgam Valley](/wiki/Trans-Karakoram_Tract "Trans-Karakoram Tract") in northeastern [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir")) with the provision that the settlement was subject to the final solution of the [Kashmir dispute](/wiki/Kashmir_conflict "Kashmir conflict") between [India](/wiki/India "India") and Pakistan.

### Since 1970

In 1970, the second military [President](/wiki/President_of_Pakistan "President of Pakistan"), [Yahya Khan](/wiki/Yahya_Khan "Yahya Khan"), abolished the political structure of West Pakistan and established four new provinces: [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh"), Punjab, [Balochistan](/wiki/Balochistan,_Pakistan "Balochistan, Pakistan") and the North-West Frontier Province. In 1971, the [Bengali](/wiki/Bengalis "Bengalis")-majority wing of East Pakistan [seceded](/wiki/Secession "Secession") from the Pakistani union following the [Bangladesh Liberation War](/wiki/Bangladesh_Liberation_War "Bangladesh Liberation War"), consequently forming the independent [People's Republic of Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"). In 1974, the remaining princely states of [Hunza](/wiki/Hunza_(princely_state) "Hunza (princely state)") and [Nagar](/wiki/Nagar_(princely_state) "Nagar (princely state)") were abolished and their territories merged into the [Gilgit Agency](/wiki/Gilgit_Agency "Gilgit Agency"), following which the [Northern Areas](/wiki/Gilgit-Baltistan "Gilgit-Baltistan") were formed. In 1975, portions of the districts of [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar_District "Peshawar District") and [Dera Ismail Khan](/wiki/Dera_Ismail_Khan_District "Dera Ismail Khan District") were separated to form the [Federally Administered Tribal Areas](/wiki/Federally_Administered_Tribal_Areas "Federally Administered Tribal Areas"). In 1981, the region surrounding Islamabad was separated from Punjab province, and renamed to the [Islamabad Capital Territory](/wiki/Islamabad_Capital_Territory "Islamabad Capital Territory").

In August 2000, [divisions](/wiki/Divisions_of_Pakistan "Divisions of Pakistan") were abolished as part of a plan to restructure [local governments](/wiki/Local_government_in_Pakistan "Local government in Pakistan"), followed by elections in 2001. Many of the functions previously handled at a provincial level had been transferred to individual [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Pakistan "Districts of Pakistan") and [tehsils](/wiki/Tehsil "Tehsil"). In 2008, the government restored the former divisions and appointed commissioners.

In 2009, the Northern Areas were renamed to Gilgit-Baltistan, and retained its formal status as an [autonomous territory](/wiki/Autonomous_administrative_division "Autonomous administrative division").[[15]](#cite_note-17)[[16]](#cite_note-18) In 2010, the North-West Frontier Province was formally renamed to [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa").[[17]](#cite_note-19) In 2018, the [National Assembly of Pakistan](/wiki/National_Assembly_of_Pakistan "National Assembly of Pakistan") and [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Provincial Assembly](/wiki/Provincial_Assembly_of_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Provincial Assembly of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") passed the historic *FATA Merger Bill* with the [Twenty-Fifth Constitutional Amendment](/wiki/Twenty-fifth_Amendment_to_the_Constitution_of_Pakistan "Twenty-fifth Amendment to the Constitution of Pakistan"). On 31 May 2018, the final step in the merger of the Federally Administered Tribal Areas with Khyber Pakhtunkhwa was completed, as then-President [Mamnoon Hussain](/wiki/Mamnoon_Hussain "Mamnoon Hussain") signed the 25th Constitutional Amendment Bill into law. The amendment's signing abolished the Federally Administered Tribal Areas as a separate political entity and merged them into the province of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa.[[18]](#cite_note-20)[[19]](#cite_note-21)[[20]](#cite_note-22)

The Provinces and administrative territories of Pakistan are subdivided into administrative "divisions", Divisional Commissioner is the administrative head of a division. Divisional Commissioner is appointed by the government of Pakistan from [Pakistan Administrative Service](/wiki/Pakistan_Administrative_Service "Pakistan Administrative Service")

### District

Main article: [Districts of Pakistan](/wiki/Districts_of_Pakistan "Districts of Pakistan")

The District Coordination Officer is the administrative head of the District Administration. They have wide-ranging responsibility for overseeing, improving and directing the approved plans of the District Government.[[21]](#cite_note-23)

The Zila Nazim used to be the executive head of the District Administration until 2010 when the government gave their powers to the District Coordination Officers also. Their role is similar to district [governor](/wiki/Governor "Governor") or [prefect](/wiki/Prefect "Prefect"), with responsibility for implementing government strategy and developing initiatives arising out of it.[[22]](#cite_note-24)

In order to decentralize administrative and financial authority to be accountable to Local Governments, for good governance, effective delivery of services, and transparent decision making through institutionalized participation of the people at grassroots level, elections to the local government institutions are held after every four years on none party basis by the Chief Election Commissioner of Pakistan.

### Tehsil

Main article: [Tehsils of Pakistan](/wiki/Tehsils_of_Pakistan "Tehsils of Pakistan")

Among the three tiers of local government, tehsil government is the second tier. It is where the functions, responsibilities, and authorities of districts government are divided into smaller units, these units are known as "tehsils". The tehsils are used all over Pakistan except Sindh province where the word "taluka" is used instead, although the functions and authorities are the same. The head of the Tehsil government is "Tehsil Nazim" who is assisted by the tehsil Naib-Nazim. Every tehsil has a [Tehsil Municipal Administration](/wiki/Tehsil_Municipal_Administration "Tehsil Municipal Administration"), consisting of a tehsil council, Tehsil Nazim, tehsil/taluka municipal officer (TMO), chief officer and other officials of the local council.[[23]](#cite_note-25)

Members of the union council including Union Administrator and Vice Union Administrator are elected through direct elections based on adult franchise and on the basis of joint electorate. However, for the election to the reserved seats for women in Zila Council proportionately divided among tehsils or towns shall be all members of the union councils in a tehsil or town. It is the responsibility of the Chief Election Commissioner to organize and conduct these elections.

### (iii) Princely States of Pakistan

Main article: [Princely states of Pakistan](/wiki/Princely_states_of_Pakistan "Princely states of Pakistan")

Between August 1947 and March 1948, the rulers of the following [princely states](/wiki/Princely_states "Princely states") (which had existed alongside but outside [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India")) [acceded](/wiki/Instrument_of_Accession "Instrument of Accession") their states to Pakistan, giving up control of their external affairs, while all retaining internal self-government, at least to begin with. This was lost by stages, until by 1974 all of the states had been fully integrated into Pakistan.
